[MCT | COMMENTARY] Investigating Sara Duterte's Confidential Fund Claims: A Political Dodge of Her Father's Legacy

Sara Duterte opened her mouth again.

Naturally, any self-respecting, nation-loving Filipino should question it—especially in a time when corruption is rampant, investigated, and still thriving.

Vice President Sara Duterte's claim that she used Department of Education (DepEd) confidential funds to investigate the ₱2.4-billion laptop corruption scandal raises more questions than answers.

She said this investigation happened during her tenure as DepEd Secretary from 2022 to 2024. But the laptop scandal she referred to happened in 2021—under her father's administration.

🔴 The Laptop Scandal Under Duterte
The ₱2.4-billion procurement happened in 2021, when the Department of Education was headed by Leonor Magtolis Briones, a Duterte appointee who led the agency from June 30, 2016, to June 30, 2022.

Laptops were purchased at ₱58,300 each, despite an approved budget of only ₱35,000.

The deal was handled by the Department of Budget and Management's Procurement Service (PS-DBM) under the Bayanihan 2 program.

The Commission on Audit later called them "outdated and pricey," running on obsolete Celeron processors unfit for distance learning.

Former Ombudsman Samuel Martires filed graft and falsification charges in 2023 against Briones and former Budget Undersecretary Christopher Lao over this procurement.

The contractor was Sunwest Construction and Development Corporation, founded by former Ako Bicol Representative Zaldy Co.

🔴 Sara's Contradictions
When Sara took over as Education Secretary in June 2022, she made a striking decision: she appointed Briones as her official consultant.

In her inaugural remarks, she called Briones "a force" and said she was "humbled and honored to follow the footsteps of a much-admired academic, public servant, social activist, and changemaker."

If she truly believed there was rampant corruption in the department, why would she hire the same person tied to that overpriced deal?

She also claimed that Zaldy Co and Sunwest were involved in the corruption she was investigating. Yet, it was during her father’s administration that Sunwest bagged the deal.

Furthermore, she said her "investigation" using confidential funds led to the filing of cases. But the cases against Briones and Lao were based on a COA report and a Senate investigation—not her confidential funds.

In fact, it was the Senate Blue Ribbon Committee, then chaired by Senator Francis Tolentino, that recommended the filing of charges. Sara's "investigation" seems to be a case of taking credit for work already done by others.

🔴 The Question of Timing
Why did Sara only mention this "investigation" now?

When the laptop scandal was being investigated by the Senate in 2022, Sara was silent. She didn't say, "Wait, I'm using my confidential funds to look into this."

Instead, she defended her consultant, Briones.

She didn't mention any "confidential fund investigation" when she was asking for more confidential funds in 2023. She didn't mention it when COA was flagging her ₱125-million spending in 11 days.

She only mentioned it after Zaldy Co, now the House Appropriations Chair, began questioning her 2025 budget and her past spending.

She even admitted her "investigation" was informal. She told reporters: "Hindi siya formal investigation... ginamit namin ang confidential funds para sa imbestigasyon na 'yun."

This revelation came only in October 2025, more than a year after she left DepEd, and just as she was under heavy scrutiny for her own confidential-fund spending.

It's worth recalling that she had already ordered a probe in August 2022, weeks after assuming office.

So if a formal investigation was already underway, why were confidential funds—meant strictly for surveillance and intelligence operations—used for something that could have been handled through the usual administrative and audit channels?

🔴 The Question Left Hanging
The laptop scandal happened during her father's presidency. His Cabinet, his appointees, his budget department, his pandemic program.

Did Sara ever ask what he knew about it? Did she coordinate with him about investigating his officials? Did she question why his Education Secretary approved such a costly deal?

If she truly sought accountability, shouldn't it start with the people who ran government at that time?

🔴 Confidential Funds and the Law
Confidential funds are meant for surveillance or intelligence activities that support national security.

At least 80 percent must go to field operations. Using them for a DepEd laptop probe doesn't meet that standard.

Shouldn't that already be an indication of technical malversation—public funds used for an unauthorized purpose?

And considering the amount, shouldn't this be examined as possible plunder?

The proper channels for investigating procurement irregularities were already in place: the Ombudsman, the Commission on Audit, and the Department of Justice. None of them require intelligence funds.

🔴 The Fake-Name Recipients
The Philippine Statistics Authority confirmed that 405 out of 677 supposed recipients of Sara's confidential funds don't exist in any registry.

Names like Mary Grace Piattos, Chippy McDonald, Fernando Tempura, and Carlos Oishi appeared in her fund records. These are obviously made-up identities.

If those funds were truly used for the laptop investigation, why are the recipients fictional? Shouldn't this already trigger a criminal probe for falsification?

🔴 COA's Findings
The Commission on Audit has already issued Notices of Disallowance for several of Sara's confidential fund expenditures:
• 2022 OVP: ₱73.2 million disallowed
• 2023 OVP: ₱67 million under review
• 2023 DepEd: ₱112.5 million under review

Sara refused to explain, saying disclosure would "compromise intelligence operations." But that excuse collapses when more than half of the supposed recipients don't exist.

🔴 Politics Beneath the Statement
Her October 2025 statement came right when Congress was probing her own confidential-fund violations.

Suddenly, she claimed those funds were used for an anti-corruption investigation.

She named Zaldy Co and Sunwest, yet Co had already been implicated in other corruption cases years earlier. Why spend intelligence funds chasing a man already under investigation?

Meanwhile, the official who approved the laptop purchase was sitting comfortably as her consultant.

🔴 The Legal Weight
Each fake acknowledgment receipt represents falsification of a public document. Hundreds of them.
Using confidential funds for something outside intelligence operations fits the definition of technical malversation. Given the scale, shouldn't investigators already be examining this as possible plunder?

🔴 The Bigger Accountability Question
The laptop deal began under Rodrigo Duterte’s government, implemented by his officials, and paid with public money. If Sara truly wanted transparency, shouldn't she have started by asking her own father what he knew?

Until she does, every statement about corruption sounds less like truth-telling and more like self-preservation.

🔴 Political Self-Preservation Disguised as Integrity
Sara Duterte’s version doesn’t hold:
She hired the very person tied to the corruption she claims to have investigated.
She used confidential funds for something outside their legal scope.
Sixty percent of her fund recipients are fake.
And this supposed investigation only surfaced when she was already being questioned for misuse of funds.

If she truly wanted accountability, she’d answer the simplest question of all:
What did her father know about the overpriced DepEd laptops bought during his presidency?

Until that question is answered—and until she explains where the confidential funds really went—her words remain a political dodge. She is not fighting corruption; she is managing a narrative.

By shifting the blame to Zaldy Co, she attempts to cast herself as a crusader while protecting the very system that allowed the laptop deal to happen under her father's watch. It is an attempt to turn a scandal into a performance. And yet, she still expects people to believe she's the victim of politics.

I've learned that with Sara, silence says more than her words ever will. Because when the truth threatens power, the first instinct isn't to explain — it's to protect.
